1: Chapter 32 is one of the most dramatically ironic passages in the Hebrew Bible: Jeremiah purchases a field while the Babylonian army is besieging Jerusalem, acting as a sign-act of restoration hope precisely when all visible evidence points to national extinction. The word that came to Jeremiah from YHWH in the tenth year of Zedekiah king of Judah, which was the eighteenth year of Nebuchadnezzar. The date is 588/587 BC — the final siege of Jerusalem. Nebuchadnezzar began the siege in Zedekiah's ninth year (2 Kings 25:1) and took the city in his tenth year (Jeremiah 39:1–2). Jeremiah is purchasing real estate during the last months before Jerusalem's fall.
2: At that time the army of the king of Babylon was besieging Jerusalem, and Jeremiah the prophet was shut up in the court of the guard that was in the palace of the king of Judah. The setting is doubly confined: the city is besieged (enclosed by the Babylonian army) and Jeremiah is imprisoned (enclosed in the palace guard-court). The prophet who counseled surrender is under house arrest, the city he prophesied would fall is being besieged. His prophetic word is being fulfilled in real time around him.
3: For Zedekiah king of Judah had imprisoned him, saying, Why do you prophesy and say, Thus says YHWH: Behold, I am giving this city into the hand of the king of Babylon, and he shall capture it. Jeremiah's imprisonment is a direct consequence of his prophetic message. Zedekiah has imprisoned him for the same reason Jehoiakim wanted to kill Uriah (Jeremiah 26:20–23): the prophet announced surrender as YHWH's will, which amounted to treason in a time of active resistance. The charge is accurate — Jeremiah did say exactly this.
4: Zedekiah king of Judah shall not escape out of the hand of the Chaldeans, but shall surely be given into the hand of the king of Babylon, and shall speak with him face to face and see him eye to eye. The specific oracle about Zedekiah's personal fate: he will be captured and brought face-to-face with Nebuchadnezzar. This was historically fulfilled — Zedekiah was captured near Jericho, brought to Nebuchadnezzar's headquarters at Riblah, watched his sons executed, was blinded, and taken to Babylon (2 Kings 25:4–7; Jeremiah 39:4–7). Face-to-face with the king, as Jeremiah predicted.
5: And he shall take Zedekiah to Babylon, and there he shall remain until I visit him, declares YHWH. Though you fight against the Chaldeans, you shall not succeed. The covenant visit (paqad) of YHWH upon Zedekiah in Babylon — a visit for mercy or judgment, the text is deliberately ambiguous. Though you fight against the Chaldeans, you shall not succeed: resistance is futile. The declaration of futility applies to all military options — no strategy, no alliance, no desperate last-stand will change what YHWH has determined.
6: Jeremiah said, The word of YHWH came to me: Hanamel the son of Shallum your uncle is going to come to you and say, Buy my field that is at Anathoth, for the right of redemption by purchase is yours. The divine foreknowledge confirmed: YHWH tells Jeremiah that his cousin Hanamel will come to him with a field-redemption offer. When Hanamel actually arrives (v8), this prediction is Jeremiah's confirmation that the purchase is YHWH's instruction, not mere family obligation. The anticipation of the visit functions as a prophetic sign: the one who can predict family visits can be trusted with promises about the nation's future.
7: Then Hanamel my cousin came to me in the court of the guard, in accordance with the word of YHWH, and said to me, Buy my field that is at Anathoth in the land of Benjamin, for the right of possession and redemption is yours; buy it for yourself. Hanamel's arrival precisely as predicted confirms that the subsequent field purchase is a divinely commanded sign-act, not merely family business. The right of redemption (mishpat hage'ulah) is the kinsman-redeemer provision of Leviticus 25:25: when a clan member is forced to sell his land, the nearest kin has the right (and obligation) to buy it and keep it within the family. Jeremiah is the appropriate redeemer for Hanamel's distressed property.
8: Then I knew that this was the word of YHWH. The confirmatory function of the prediction: Hanamel's arrival validates the divine origin of the purchase command. The prophet who was uncertain whether to comply with an apparently bizarre instruction (buy land during a siege?) finds in the fulfilled prediction the confirmation he needed. This is an important insight into how prophetic guidance worked in practice: confirmation through fulfilled prior knowledge.
9: And I bought the field at Anathoth from Hanamel my cousin, and weighed out the money to him, seventeen shekels of silver. The actual transaction: seventeen shekels of silver for the Anathoth field. This is a real price for a real property transaction — archaeologists have found ancient Near Eastern field sale contracts with comparable amounts. The specific price details authenticate the historicity of the transaction. Jeremiah pays from whatever resources he has while imprisoned, investing in property in a city about to fall to an enemy who will level it.
10: I signed the deed, sealed it, got witnesses, and weighed the money on scales. The legal procedure is scrupulous: signature, sealing, witnesses, and weighed silver. This is authentic ancient Near Eastern real estate protocol. The meticulous legal formality in the midst of siege and imprisonment makes the transaction even more remarkable — Jeremiah is performing the full legal requirements for a valid property transfer as if future enforcement of the deed were fully expected.
11: Then I took the sealed deed of purchase, containing the terms and conditions and the open copy. Ancient Near Eastern property deeds were typically written in two copies on a single clay tablet: the sealed copy (protected against tampering) and an open copy (accessible for reference). The sealed copy would be stored in a clay jar; the open copy could be consulted when needed. This dual-copy system provided both security and accessibility — Jeremiah is following established legal practice.
12: And I gave the deed of purchase to Baruch the son of Neriah son of Mahseiah, in the presence of Hanamel my cousin, in the presence of the witnesses who signed the deed of purchase, and in the presence of all the Judeans who were sitting in the court of the guard. Baruch ben Neriah is Jeremiah's scribe and primary disciple — the one who wrote down Jeremiah's oracles (Jeremiah 36:4), read them in the Temple (Jeremiah 36:6–10), and eventually compiled/edited the book of Jeremiah. His custody of the deed of purchase places the most important sign-act in Jeremiah's ministry in the hands of the man who will preserve Jeremiah's entire prophetic legacy.
13: And I charged Baruch in their presence, saying. Jeremiah delivers specific instructions to Baruch regarding the deed's preservation — the instructions that follow explain the purpose of the entire sign-act.
14: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: Take these deeds, both this sealed deed of purchase and this open deed, and put them in an earthenware vessel, that they may last for a long time. Clay jars preserve documents extremely well — the Dead Sea Scrolls, preserved in ceramic jars for 2,000 years, demonstrate the effectiveness of this archival method. The instruction to preserve the deed for a long time is itself the theological point: the deed will need to be kept long enough for conditions to change, for the siege to end, for Babylon to fall, for exiles to return and claim their ancestral properties. The jar-preservation anticipates a long wait.
15: For thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: Houses and fields and vineyards shall again be bought in this land. The theological explanation of the entire sign-act, delivered in the most economically absurd possible moment. While the city is besieged, its houses being reduced to rubble, its fields lying fallow, YHWH declares: normal economic life will return. Fields will be bought and sold. The deed Jeremiah is filing away in a clay jar represents a future transaction that the current generation may not live to see but that YHWH guarantees will happen.
16: After I had given the deed of purchase to Baruch the son of Neriah, I prayed to YHWH, saying. Jeremiah's prayer follows the sign-act — a pattern of action then theological reflection. The prayer (vv16–25) is remarkable for its honest tension: it begins with confident doxology and ends with a question that essentially asks, why did you tell me to do this?
17: Ah, Lord YHWH! It is you who have made the heavens and the earth by your great power and by your outstretched arm. Nothing is too hard for you. The prayer opens with creation theology: YHWH made heaven and earth by his great power and outstretched arm — the Exodus-warfare language applied to creation. Nothing is too hard for you (lo-yippale' mimkha kol-davar): the theological affirmation that grounds all petition. The God who created the cosmos can restore a devastated nation — the restoration promise is not beyond his power.
18: You show steadfast love to thousands, but you repay the guilt of fathers to their children after them, O great and mighty God, whose name is YHWH of hosts. The Exodus doxology of Exodus 34:6–7 echoes here: YHWH showing chesed to thousands while visiting iniquity on the third and fourth generation. Jeremiah has lived through the fulfillment of both aspects: he has seen the steadfast love of YHWH's covenant promises and the visiting of the fathers' guilt in the exile of his own generation. Both are real; both are YHWH's.
19: Great in counsel and mighty in deed, whose eyes are open to all the ways of the children of man, rewarding each one according to his ways and according to the fruit of his deeds. The divine omniscience and moral recompense formula: YHWH sees every human way and action, and his reward/punishment is calibrated accordingly (according to his ways and the fruit of his deeds). This is the covenant framework applied at the cosmic level: the God who judges nations by this standard is also the God who restores nations by his free grace.
20: Who performed signs and wonders in the land of Egypt, and to this day in Israel and among all mankind, and have made a name for yourself, as at this day. The Exodus signs and wonders (otot umoftim) as the paradigm of divine intervention in history. To this day: the Exodus events were not ancient history but living memory, the continuing basis of Israel's identity and YHWH's ongoing reputation among the nations. The God whose name was made great in Egypt is the same God who can make his name great through Israel's restoration from Babylon.
21: You brought your people Israel out of the land of Egypt with signs and wonders, with a strong hand and outstretched arm, and with great terror. The Exodus vocabulary accumulates: signs and wonders, strong hand, outstretched arm, great terror (mora gadol). This is the Deuteronomic confession (Deuteronomy 26:8) quoted almost verbatim. Jeremiah is embedding his prayer in Israel's founding narrative — the same God who did all this in Egypt is the one being addressed now.
22: And you gave them this land, which you swore to their fathers to give them, a land flowing with milk and honey. The Patriarchal promise and its Exodus-era fulfillment: the land oath sworn to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (Genesis 15:18–21; Jeremiah 28:13–15) was executed in the Conquest. The land given is now being taken away in exile — but the original gift was a covenant act of YHWH, and the exile does not cancel the covenant. The land-gift grounds the hope of restoration.
23: And they entered and took possession of it. But they did not obey your voice or walk in your law. They did nothing of all you commanded them to do. Therefore you have made all this disaster come upon them. The audit of covenant history from possession through violation to disaster: receive, disobey, suffer. The logic is consistent and transparent. Jeremiah's prayer does not suppress the covenant accounting — it faces it directly. The disaster is YHWH's just response to covenant violation; acknowledging this is essential to genuine prayer.
24: Behold, the siege mounds have come up to the city to take it, and the city is given into the hands of the Chaldeans who are fighting against it, because of sword and famine and pestilence. What you spoke has come to pass, and behold, you see it. The present visible reality: the siege works are in place, the city is falling, the judgment triad is active. What you spoke has come to pass: the prophetic word has been fulfilled. Jeremiah is noting that YHWH's announcements have proven accurate — which is both a confirmation of prophetic authority and a preparation for the question that follows.
25: Yet you, O Lord YHWH, have said to me, Buy the field for money and get witnesses — though the city is given into the hands of the Chaldeans. The honest tension of the prayer surfaces: the same YHWH who announced the city's fall has commanded Jeremiah to buy a field in the falling city. The contradiction is real and admitted. Jeremiah is not pretending to understand YHWH's logic; he is pressing the apparent incoherence back to the one who created it. This is the prayer of authentic faith: confessing incomprehension while maintaining trust.
26: The word of YHWH came to Jeremiah: Behold, I am YHWH, the God of all flesh. Is anything too hard for me? YHWH's response begins with a question that echoes and completes Jeremiah's own affirmation (nothing is too hard for you, v17). The title God of all flesh (Elohei kol-basar): YHWH's domain is every living creature — he is not limited to Israel, to the covenant land, or to the Temple. Is anything too hard for me? — the rhetorical question that frames everything that follows. The field purchase in a besieged city seems impossible as a meaningful investment; YHWH asserts his power over every apparent impossibility.
27: YHWH's self-identification as God of all flesh establishes the scope of his authority. The restoration of a nation, the rebuilding of a city, the return of exiles from across the known world — all of these fall within the domain of the God who governs all living things. The question is anything too hard for me? is not rhetorical posturing but a direct invitation to trust: bring your hardest cases and watch what I can do.
28: Therefore, thus says YHWH: Behold, I am giving this city into the hands of the Chaldeans and into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, and he shall capture it. YHWH's answer to Jeremiah's prayer-question begins with the judgment: yes, the city will fall. The affirmation of Jeremiah's prophetic announcement about Jerusalem's fall (the same announcement that got him imprisoned) comes directly from YHWH. There is no miraculous reversal at the last moment. The judgment is real, coming, and irreversible.
29: The Chaldeans who are fighting against this city shall come and set this city on fire and burn it, with the houses on whose rooftops offerings have been made to Baal and drink offerings have been poured out to other gods, to provoke me to anger. The burning is specified, and the reason is given: the rooftop altars to Baal and other gods (cf. Jeremiah 19:13) that covered Jerusalem's residential architecture. The city is burned not by Babylonian capriciousness but as the direct result of the apostasy that the houses themselves witnessed and hosted.
30: For the children of Israel and the children of Judah have done nothing but evil in my sight from their youth. The children of Israel have done nothing but provoke me to anger by the work of their hands, declares YHWH. The comprehensive audit: from their youth (mina'ureihem) — from the beginning of the nation's existence — both Israel and Judah have engaged in covenant violation. The work of their hands (idols) is the consistent trigger of divine anger throughout the book. The judgment is not a recent decision but the culmination of a long accumulation.
31: This city has aroused my anger and wrath, from the day it was built to this day, so that I will remove it from my sight. The city of Jerusalem itself — from its Davidic founding — has been a source of covenant failure rather than covenant faithfulness. The Temple that was supposed to be the place of divine presence and covenant maintenance became the location of covenant violation. Remove it from my sight: the departure of the divine presence from Jerusalem, which Ezekiel sees in his vision (Ezek 10–11), is the inner meaning of the city's fall.
32: Because of all the evil of the children of Israel and the children of Judah that they did to provoke me to anger — their kings, their officials, their priests, their prophets, the men of Judah, and the inhabitants of Jerusalem. The comprehensive social indictment: every stratum of society (kings, officials, priests, prophets, general population) bears responsibility for the covenant failure. No group is exempt; the judgment falls on the entire social hierarchy that was supposed to maintain covenant fidelity but instead led covenant violation.
33: They have turned their back to me and not their face. And though I have taught them persistently, they have not listened to receive instruction. The back-versus-face image (cf. Jeremiah 2:27; Jeremiah 18:17): the people have turned away from YHWH in posture and in orientation. The teaching (hashem veshalloach, rising early and sending) has been persistent and urgent; the listening has been absent. The same formula as Jeremiah 7:13, Jeremiah 25:3–4 — the exhaustion of the channels of covenant communication.
34: They set up their abominations in the house that is called by my name, to defile it. The Temple desecration — the installation of foreign cult objects within the Temple precincts (cf. 2 Kings 21:4–7; Ezek 8) — is the culminating covenant violation. The house called by my name (asher niqra shemi alav): the divine name-claim on the Temple makes its desecration the most direct form of covenant insult. The abominations within YHWH's own house are the final provocation.
35: They built the high places of Baal in the Valley of the Son of Hinnom, to offer up their sons and daughters to Molech, though I did not command them, nor did it enter into my mind, that they should do this abomination. The Hinnom Valley / Topheth child sacrifice (cf. chapter 19) is again specified as the theological nadir — the crime YHWH most explicitly disclaims (did not command, nor did it enter my mind). The triple denial that appeared in 19:5 recurs here, emphasizing the absolute incompatibility of child sacrifice with Yahwistic covenant religion.
36: Now therefore thus says YHWH, the God of Israel, concerning this city of which you say, It is given into the hand of the king of Babylon by sword, by famine, and by pestilence. The pivot from judgment to restoration: now therefore introduces the second part of YHWH's response to Jeremiah's prayer. The judgment having been fully confirmed, the restoration can now be announced. The same city that is being destroyed will be the city of restoration.
37: Behold, I will gather them from all the countries to which I drove them in my anger and my wrath and in great indignation. I will bring them back to this place, and I will make them dwell in safety. The gathering from all countries — the comprehensive diaspora ingathering (cf. Jeremiah 29:14; Jeremiah 31:8) — is YHWH's act of reversing his own judgment. In my anger, my wrath, and in great indignation: the exile was fully YHWH's act; the restoration is equally fully YHWH's act. Dwell in safety: the security that the covenant community in Jeremiah's time had lost — from foreign invasion and internal social breakdown — will be restored.
38: And they shall be my people, and I will be their God. The covenant formula at the heart of the entire Book of Consolation. The sixth time this formula appears in Jeremiah (Jeremiah 7:23; Jeremiah 11:4; Jeremiah 24:7; Jeremiah 30:22; Jeremiah 31:1, 33; Jeremiah 32:38) — each occurrence marking a stage in the covenant-judgment-restoration arc. Here it is the anchor of the restoration promise: the relationship that was strained to the breaking point by exile is fully reconstituted.
39: I will give them one heart and one way, that they fear me forever, for their own good and the good of their children after them. The one heart (lev echad) is the undivided heart of covenant fidelity — the opposite of the divided heart that pursued both YHWH and idols. This is the New Covenant promise of Jeremiah 31:33 (internalized Torah) expressed in different vocabulary: a heart that is unified in its orientation toward YHWH. One way (derek echad): the single covenant path rather than the many wandering paths of apostasy (cf. Jeremiah 6:16 — the ancient paths).
40: I will make with them an everlasting covenant, that I will not turn away from doing good to them. And I will put the fear of me in their hearts, that they may not turn from me. The everlasting covenant (berith olam) is the New Covenant of Jeremiah 31:31–34 described with permanence language: YHWH commits never to turn away from doing good. The internal covenant-keeping (the fear of me in their hearts) addresses the root failure of the Sinai covenant: the people kept turning from YHWH because their hearts were not secured to him. The New Covenant secures the heart from the inside.
41: I will rejoice in doing them good, and I will plant them in this land in faithfulness, with all my heart and all my soul. With all my heart and all my soul (bechol-libbi uvechol-nafshi): the covenant formula of Deuteronomy 6:5 (love YHWH with all your heart and soul) is applied to YHWH's own commitment to Israel. The God who commanded Israel to love him wholeheartedly will himself love Israel wholeheartedly in the restoration. The restoration is not a reluctant act of divine obligation but a joyful expression of divine love.
42: For thus says YHWH: Just as I have brought all this great disaster upon this people, so I will bring upon them all the good that I promise them. The symmetry of YHWH's covenant administration: the same certainty that characterized the disaster will characterize the restoration. The judgment was not exaggerated; neither is the promise. The people who watched the siege works rise around their city can be equally certain that the restoration promises will be executed with equal thoroughness.
43: Fields shall be bought in this land of which you are saying, It is a desolation, without man or beast; it is given into the hand of the Chaldeans. The direct connection to the sign-act: the fields-will-be-bought promise (v15) is here expanded into the full restoration context. The field Jeremiah purchased is not merely a personal investment but a prophetic deposit — the first transaction in a future market of covenant-land activity that YHWH guarantees will flourish.
44: Fields shall be bought for money, and deeds shall be signed and sealed and witnessed, in the land of Benjamin, in the places around Jerusalem, and in the cities of Judah, in the cities of the hill country, in the cities of the Shephelah, and in the cities of the Negeb; for I will restore their fortunes, declares YHWH. The geographic completeness of the restoration: Benjamin (where Anathoth is), Jerusalem's surroundings, the cities of Judah, the hill country, the Shephelah (coastal lowlands), the Negeb (southern desert fringe). Every region of the covenant land will be restored to normal economic activity — property transactions, legal deeds, witnesses. The meticulous legal procedure Jeremiah performed in a prison courtyard (vv9–12) will become the ordinary practice of a restored community throughout every corner of the land.
